sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 66, level 3.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 3 of about 86 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
All 8 targets are already "covered" by the structure (within 1 cell). But the level isn't completed yet. The targets need to be exactly on the structure's arms, not just nearby. Let me think about this differently.
Looking at the structure:
- X center at ~(43, 18) - wait, the centroid says (43.2, 17.7) but that includes the line pixels. Let me recalculate.
- Actually, the X has 48 pixels and the diamond has 50 pixels.
Wait, the X centroid is at (43.2, 17.7) which is above row 45. But the X shape visually is centered around row 47-48. The centroid calculation is being skewed because the X pixels include the diagonal arms extending upward.
Let me reconsider the structure. The X has arms going in 4 diagonal directions from its center. The diamond has edges forming a rotated square. The horizontal line connects them.
The key question is: what's the goal? Looking at level 1 and 2 patterns, the goal is to move the structure so that its arms pass through the target squares. When a target is "collected", it disappears or changes color.
Currently all 8 targets are still visible (charcoal squares). So I need to figure out the exact alignment needed.
Let me look at the X arm positions more carefully. The X center is where the two diagonals cross. Looking at the ascii:
- Row 45: horizontal line
- The X arms extend from row 45 diagonally
Actually, I think the X center is at (45, 18) - where the horizontal line crosses the X. The arms extend:
- Up-left from (45, 18): (44, 17), (43, 16), etc.
- Up-right from (45, 18): (44, 19), (43, 20), etc.
- Down-left from (45, 18): (46, 17), (47, 16), etc.
- Down-right from (45, 18): (46, 19), (47, 20), etc.
Wait, but I moved the structure UP once. Before that, the horizontal line was at row 44. After moving up, it's at row 45. So the structure moved up.
Let me check what the X arms look like and see which targets they should hit. The targets are at specific positions, and the X arms form diagonal lines. I need to position the structure so that the arms pass through the target centers.
Let me look at the target positions relative to the X center:
- (6, 6): row diff = 6-45 = -39, col diff = 6-18 = -12 → not on a diagonal
- (21, 21): row diff = 21-45 = -24, col diff = 21-18 = 3 → not on a diagonal
- (27, 9): row diff = 27-45 = -18, col diff = 9-18 = -9 → not on a diagonal
- (39, 21): row diff = 39-45 = -6, col diff = 21-18 = 3 → not on a diagonal
Hmm, these don't seem to align with the X arms at the current position. But maybe the structure needs to be moved to a specific position where the arms DO align.
Actually wait - the targets are 3x3 squares, not single points. And the structure arms are 1-pixel wide lines. So "covering" means the arm passes through any part of the 3x3 square.
Let me reconsider. The X arms extend diagonally at 45 degrees. For a target to be on an X arm, the target center needs to satisfy |row - center_row| = |col - center_col| (for the diagonal lines).
Let me check if there's a position where multiple targets align with the X arms.
Actually, I realize I should look at this more carefully. The X has arms that extend in 4 directions. The diamond has 4 edges. The horizontal line connects them. The targets need to be "collected" by having the structure's arms pass through them.
Let me think about which targets belong to which structure:
- X targets (left side): (6, 6), (21, 21), (27, 9), (39, 21)
- Diamond targets (right side): (6, 45), (15, 51), (30, 48), (33, 33)
For the X, the arms are at 45° diagonals. If the X center is at (r, c), then:
- Up-left arm: (r-k, c-k) for k = 1, 2, ...
- Up-right arm: (r-k, c+k) for k = 1, 2, ...
- Down-left arm: (r+k, c-k) for k = 1, 2, ...
- Down-right arm: (r+k, c+k) for k = 1, 2, ...
For a target at (tr, tc) to be on an arm: |tr - r| = |tc - c|
Let me check the left-side targets:
- (6, 6): |6-r| = |6-c|
- (21, 21): |21-r| = |21-c|
- (27, 9): |27-r| = |9-c|
- (39, 21): |39-r| = |21-c|
For (6, 6) and (21, 21) to both be on arms:
- 6-r = 6-c → r = c (if both on same diagonal direction)
- 21-r = 21-c → r = c (same)
So if r = c, then (6, 6) and (21, 21) are both on the up-left arm (if r > 21).
For (27, 9) and (39, 21):
- |27-r| = |9-c| and |39-r| = |21-c|
- If r = c: |27-r| = |9-r| and |39-r| = |21-r|
- 27-r = -(9-r) → 27-r = r-9 → 2r = 36 → r = 18
- Check: |39-18| = 21, |21-18| = 3 → 21 ≠ 3, so this doesn't work
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the targets don't all need to be on the same arm. Each target could be on a different arm.
For (6, 6) on up-left: 6 < r and 6 < c, so r - 6 = c - 6, meaning r = c
For (21, 21) on up-left: 21 < r and 21 < c, so r - 21 = c - 21, meaning r = c
For (27, 9) on up-left: 27 < r and 9 < c, so r - 27 = c - 9, meaning r - c = 18
For (39, 21) on up-right: 39 < r and 21 > c, so r - 39 = 21 - c, meaning r + c = 60
If r = c (from first two targets), then:
- (27, 9) needs r - c = 18, but r = c means r - c = 0 ≠ 18
So not all targets can be on the up-left arm. Let me try different arm assignments.
For the X at center (r, c):
- (6, 6): up-left if r > 6 and c > 6, so r - 6 = c - 6, r = c
- (21, 21): up-left if r > 21 and c > 21, so r = c
- (27, 9): up-left if r > 27 and c > 9, so r - 27 = c - 9, r - c = 18
  OR up-right if r > 27 and c < 9, so r - 27 = 9 - c, r + c = 36
  OR down-left if r < 27 and c > 9, so 27 - r = c - 9, r + c = 36
  OR down-right if r < 27 and c < 9, so 27 - r = 9 - c, r - c = 18
- (39, 21): similar analysis
This is getting complex. Let me just try moving the structure and see what happens when it passes through targets.
Actually, I already moved UP once and all targets are still there. Let me try pressing SPACE to see if I can switch to the diamond and move it separately.
Wait, actually I realize something. After pressing SPACE, the two structures merged into one (50 pixels for the X structure). When I moved UP, the whole thing moved. But the cursor disappeared from the structure.
Let me check: is the cursor still on the structure? The white pixel I found was at (63, 63) which is on the HUD bar. So the cursor is no longer on the structure.
Maybe I need to press SPACE again to reactivate the cursor on the structure. Or maybe the cursor needs to be repositioned.
Let me try pressing SPACE to see what happens.

sections.content:

<parameter=code>

action(['SPACE'])

</tool_call>
